title: What is a Digital Image?
concept: A digital image is a grid of tiny squares called pixels, and every pixel is just a number (or a few numbers) telling a screen how bright or what color to show.

exercises.prompt: What do you think a photo on your phone is actually made of, underneath the picture you see?

options.text: A grid of numbers arranged in rows and columns
A single number for the whole picture
A tiny paragraph of text describing the scene

exercises.explain: Right! Every digital image is secretly a big grid of numbers — you just see it rendered as a picture.

cards.title: Zoom in far enough, and the picture disappears
cards.body: Zoom into any photo on your phone enough times, and the smooth image breaks apart into little colored squares. Keep going, and you'll hit the smallest piece possible — a single square that can't be split any further. That square is a pixel, and it's the atom of every digital image.

cards.title: An image is a grid of numbers
cards.body: A digital image is a grid of pixels ('picture elements') arranged in rows and columns. Each pixel stores a number that tells the computer how bright or what color that tiny spot should be. A whole photo is just thousands (or millions) of these numbers sitting next to each other.

cards.title: Think of it like a mosaic or a spreadsheet
cards.body: Picture an old mosaic made from thousands of small colored tiles — from far away it looks like a smooth scene, but up close it's just tiles in a grid. A digital image works the same way, except each 'tile' is a pixel, and instead of colored glass, it holds a number. You could open an image file in a spreadsheet and, in principle, see a giant grid of values.

cards.title: A tiny 4x4 image, pixel by pixel
cards.body: Imagine a black-and-white image that's just 4 pixels wide and 4 pixels tall — 16 pixels total. Each pixel might hold a number like 0 (pure black), 255 (pure white), or something in between (a shade of gray). The computer doesn't 'see' a picture at all — it just sees a small table of 16 numbers, and a screen turns those numbers into visible squares.

cards.title: A picture is not one single 'thing' to a computer
cards.body: It's tempting to think a computer looks at a photo the way we do — recognizing a dog or a sunset instantly. It doesn't. A computer only ever sees a grid of numbers. Any 'understanding' of what's in the image has to be built up from those numbers — which is exactly what the rest of computer vision is about.

cards.title: What you see vs. what the computer sees
cards.body: Same image, two totally different views of it.

left.text:
A smooth photo of a cat.
Fur, whiskers, a face.
Instantly recognizable.

right.text:
A giant grid of numbers.
No 'cat' label anywhere.
Just rows and columns of values.

exercises.prompt: What is the smallest building block of a digital image called?
exercises.explain: Correct. A pixel ('picture element') is the smallest single unit of a digital image.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A computer sees a photo the same way a human does — as a scene with objects in it.
exercises.explain: False. A computer only sees a grid of numbers; it has no built-in sense of 'cat' or 'sunset' without further processing.

exercises.sentence: A digital image is really just a ___ of numbers arranged in rows and columns.
exercises.explain: Grid is correct. Rows and columns of pixel values make up the image.

exercises.prompt: Which description most accurately explains what a digital image is at its core?

options.text: A grid of pixels, where each pixel is a number representing brightness or color
A single big number that represents the whole scene at once
A list of object names the camera detected

exercises.explain: Exactly. Every digital image, no matter how detailed, boils down to a grid of pixel numbers.

exercises.prompt: A friend says, 'A digital photo is basically a picture file, so a computer just looks at it like we do.' What's the best correction?

options.text: A digital photo is a grid of pixel numbers; the computer processes numbers, not a 'picture' the way we perceive it
The friend is right, computers see images exactly like humans do
Digital photos don't actually contain any numbers at all
Computers only understand text, never images

exercises.explain: Exactly. A digital image is fundamentally a grid of numeric pixel values — recognizing what's 'in' the picture takes extra work on top of that grid.
